title: Get started
text: SEMs ubiquitous social sciences, psychology, ecology, fields. INLAvaan package (Jamil Rue 2026b) provides user-friendly interface fitting Bayesian SEMs using Integrated Nested Laplace Approximations (INLA, Rue et al. 2009), based bespoke approximate Bayesian inference framework SEMs (Jamil Rue 2026a). vignette guide basics using INLAvaan fit simple SEM. begin make sure installed INLAvaan package GitHub running commands .
code: # Load all libraries for this example library(INLAvaan) library(tidyverse) library(lavaan)

title: Get started
text: motivate use SEMs, consider introductory example Song Lee (2012): poorer glycemic control lead greater severity kidney disease? observe three indicators glycemic control (y1y_1, y2y_2, y3y_3) three indicators kidney disease severity (y4y_4, y5y_5, y6y_6). Rather fitting separate regression models indicator, SEM allows us model relationship latent constructs , providing clearer coherent representation underlying processes. hypothesised SEM illustrated figure :

title: Get started
text: two-factor SEM described , easy simulate data using lavaan package . code : code , note true values parameters, including factor loadings Λ\Lambda, regression coefficient β\beta two latent variables, well residual latent variances Θ\Theta Ψ\Psi respectively.

title: Get started
text: Now simulated data, can fit SEM using INLAvaan. model syntax similar lavaan, making easy specify model. details model syntax, refer lavaan website. INLAvaan provides mirror functions main model fitting functions lavaan: acfa() mirrors lavaan::cfa() confirmatory factor analysis; asem() mirrors lavaan::sem() structural equation models; agrowth() mirrors lavaan::growth() latent growth curve models. code fit SEM model : lavaan::sem(), model fitted without mean structure default (meanstructure = FALSE). Rather profiling means lavaan , INLAvaan assigns saturated means flat priors marginalises analytically, keeps likelihood proper Bayesian object. Intercepts can instead modelled explicitly meanstructure = TRUE. See mean structures article two treatments relate model comparisons across meaningful. INLAvaan computes approximation posterior density way Laplace approximation (Tierney et al. 1989; Jamil Rue 2026a). joint mode Hessian needs computed, gives Gaussian distribution joint posterior parameters. default method optimisation stats::nlminb(), optimisers can used specifying optim_method = "ucminf" ucminf package optim_method = "optim" call stats::optim() function method "BFGS". , marginal posterior distributions parameter can obtained one several ways, including 1) Skew normal fitting (marginal_method = "skewnorm", default method, see Chiuchiolo et al. 2023); 2) Two-piece asymmetric Gaussian fitting (marginal_method = "asymgaus", see Martins et al. 2013); 3) Direct marginalisation joint Gaussian posterior (marginal_method = "marggaus"); 4) Sampling joint Gaussian posterior (marginal_method = "sampling"). marginal posterior distributions obtained, can use compute derived quantities interest via copula sampling. posterior predictive p-values (Gelman et al. 1996) Deviance Information Criterion (DIC, Spiegelhalter et al. 2002) computed way. default test = "standard", leave-one-cross-validation WAIC results (see model comparison section ) also computed fit time stored fit, whenever model supports additional cost small. Often, posterior sampling takes longer model fitting , number samples can controlled via nsamp argument (default nsamp = 1000), post-fitting computations can skipped altogether (test = "none").
code: mod <- "   eta1 =~ y1 + y2 + y3   eta2 =~ y4 + y5 + y6   eta2 ~ eta1 " fit <- asem(mod, dat) #> ℹ Mode finding and Hessian computation. #> ✔ Posterior mode and Hessian. [257ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.055σ. [128ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/13 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fit 13/13 skew-normal marginals. [401ms] #>  #> ℹ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). #> ✔ Adjust copula correlations (NORTA). [74ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ✔ Summarise 1000 posterior draws. [1s] #>  #> ℹ Fit measures: PPP, DIC, LOO, WAIC.

title: Get started
text: INLAvaan package uses prior specification syntax blavaan (Merkle Rosseel 2018; Merkle et al. 2021), detailed . Essentially, two ways set priors model parameters: 1) Globally parameters certain type (e.g., factor loadings, regression coefficients, etc.); 2) Individually specific parameters model syntax. default global priors similar blavaan: Note , INLAvaan uses separation strategy variance matrices, consequently places priors correlations instead covariances. , instead wished set global priors, say gamma distribution variances instead standard deviations (default), following: set individual priors specific parameters, can model syntax . instance, set normal prior mean 1 standard deviation 3 factor loading y3 eta1, normal prior mean 0 standard deviation 0.5 regression coefficient eta1 eta2, specify model follows:
code: priors_for()  # similar to blavaan::dpriors() #>                nu             alpha            lambda              beta  #>    "normal(0,32)"    "normal(0,10)"    "normal(0,10)"    "normal(0,10)"  #>             theta               psi               rho               tau  #> "gamma(1,.5)[sd]" "gamma(1,.5)[sd]"       "beta(1,1)"   "normal(0,1.5)" DP <- priors_for(theta = "gamma(1,1)", psi = "gamma(1,1)") DP #>              nu           alpha          lambda            beta           theta  #>  "normal(0,32)"  "normal(0,10)"  "normal(0,10)"  "normal(0,10)"    "gamma(1,1)"  #>             psi             rho             tau  #>    "gamma(1,1)"     "beta(1,1)" "normal(0,1.5)" ## fit <- asem(mod, dat, dpriors = DP)  # not run mod <- "   eta1 =~ y1 + y2 + prior('normal(1,3)')*y3   eta2 =~ y4 + y5 + y6   eta2 ~ prior('normal(0,.5)')*eta1 " ## fit <- asem(mod, dat)  # not run

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Classical SEM fit indices (RMSEA, CFI, TLI, etc.) computed single maximum-likelihood chi-square statistic. Bayesian SEM, parameters random, posterior draw yields different chi-square value , therefore, different set fit indices. INLAvaan provides Bayesian analogues common indices, following framework Garnier-Villarreal Jorgensen (2020) (implemented blavaan). vignette covers: Mathematical definitions Bayesian fit indices. two rescaling methods ("devM" "MCMC"). worked example Holzinger–Swineford data. Comparison baseline model (incremental indices). Differences INLAvaan blavaan.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Let 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} denote ss-th posterior draw model parameters (s=1,…,Ss = 1, \dots, S). per-sample deviance chi-square χs2=2[ℓsat−ℓ(𝛉(s))],   \chi^2_s = 2 \bigl[\ell_{\text{sat}} - \ell(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)})\bigr],  ℓsat\ell_{\text{sat}} log-likelihood saturated model (sample moments equal model moments) ℓ(𝛉(s))\ell(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)}) log-likelihood evaluated ss-th draw. equals NFML(𝛉(s))N \, F_{\text{ML}}(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)}), FMLF_{\text{ML}} ML discrepancy function. INLAvaan supports two rescaling methods, controlled rescale argument: "devM" (default). Uses DIC-based effective number parameters pDp_D. ds=χs2−pD,df=p−pD,Nadj=N.   d_s = \chi^2_s - p_D, \qquad   \mathrm{df} = p - p_D, \qquad   N_{\mathrm{adj}} = N.  pDp_D unreasonable (pD≤0p_D \leq 0 pD≥pp_D \geq p), INLAvaan falls back using pD=qp_D = q (number free parameters). "MCMC". Uses classical chi-square N−1N - 1 scaling. ds=N−1Nχs2,df=p−q,Nadj=N−G,   d_s = \frac{N - 1}{N} \chi^2_s, \qquad   \mathrm{df} = p - q, \qquad   N_{\mathrm{adj}} = N - G,  qq number free parameters GG number groups. cases per-sample noncentrality parameter λ̂s=max⁡(ds−df,0)\hat\lambda_s = \max(d_s - \mathrm{df},\, 0). following indices computed posterior draw ss, generating posterior distribution. BRMSEA (Bayesian RMSEA). BRMSEAs=λ̂sdf⋅Nadj⋅G.   \text{BRMSEA}_s = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\lambda_s}{\mathrm{df} \cdot N_\text{adj}}} \cdot \sqrt{G}. BGammaHat. BGammaHats=vv+2λ̂s/Nadj,   \text{BGammaHat}_s = \frac{v}{v + 2\hat\lambda_s / N_\text{adj}},  v=∑gpgv = \sum_g p_g total number observed variables across groups. Adjusted BGammaHat. adjBGammaHats=1−pdf(1−BGammaHats).   \text{adjBGammaHat}_s = 1 - \frac{p}{\mathrm{df}} \bigl(1 - \text{BGammaHat}_s\bigr). BMc (McDonald’s centrality index). BMcs=exp⁡(−12λ̂s/Nadj).   \text{BMc}_s = \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\hat\lambda_s / N_\text{adj}\bigr). Incremental indices compare target model baseline (null) model. Let ds(0)d_s^{(0)}, df(0)\mathrm{df}^{(0)}, λ̂s(0)\hat\lambda_s^{(0)} denote corresponding quantities baseline model. BCFI (Bayesian CFI). BCFIs=1−λ̂sλ̂s(0).   \text{BCFI}_s = 1 - \frac{\hat\lambda_s}{\hat\lambda_s^{(0)}}. BTLI (Bayesian TLI). BTLIs=ds(0)/df(0)−ds/dfds(0)/df(0)−1.   \text{BTLI}_s = \frac{d_s^{(0)} / \mathrm{df}^{(0)} - d_s / \mathrm{df}}{d_s^{(0)} / \mathrm{df}^{(0)} - 1}. BNFI (Bayesian NFI). BNFIs=ds(0)−dsds(0).   \text{BNFI}_s = \frac{d_s^{(0)} - d_s}{d_s^{(0)}}. posterior expectations (EAP), standard deviations, quantile-based credible intervals, modes distributions reported summary() method.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: fit three-factor CFA Holzinger–Swineford (1939) data. fitMeasures() returns posterior mean (EAP) Bayesian fit index alongside marginal log-likelihood, ppp, DIC, gradient diagnostics. inspect full posterior distribution index, use bfit_indices(). returns S3 object class "bfit_indices". Calling summary() provides table posterior summaries (Mean, SD, 2.5%, 50%, 97.5%, Mode) index. can also access raw per-sample vectors custom analysis:  Posterior distribution BRMSEA
code: HS.model <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 "  fit <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, verbose = FALSE) fitMeasures(fit) #>         npar   margloglik          ppp          dic        p_dic       BRMSEA  #>           21    -3841.139        0.000     7552.772       20.663        0.115  #>    BGammaHat adjBGammaHat          BMc     elpd_loo        p_loo        looic  #>        0.934        0.877        0.852    -3769.087       23.658     7538.174  #>       se_loo    elpd_waic       p_waic         waic      se_waic  #>       85.999    -3769.268       23.280     7538.536       85.865 bfi <- bfit_indices(fit) bfi #> Posterior summary of devM-based Bayesian fit indices (nsamp = 1000):  #>  #>       BRMSEA    BGammaHat adjBGammaHat          BMc  #>        0.115        0.933        0.877        0.852 summary(bfi) #>  #> Posterior summary of devM-based Bayesian fit indices (nsamp = 1000): #>  #>               Mean    SD X2.5.  X25.  X50.  X75. X97.5.  Mode #> BRMSEA       0.115 0.004 0.108 0.112 0.114 0.117  0.123 0.114 #> BGammaHat    0.933 0.004 0.924 0.931 0.934 0.936  0.940 0.935 #> adjBGammaHat 0.877 0.008 0.860 0.872 0.878 0.883  0.890 0.880 #> BMc          0.852 0.010 0.831 0.846 0.853 0.858  0.867 0.855 hist(bfi$indices$BRMSEA, breaks = 30, main = "BRMSEA", xlab = "BRMSEA",      col = "steelblue", border = "white")

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: rescale argument controls chi-square degrees freedom computed. default "devM", subtracts DIC-based pDp_D deviance. use classical N−1N - 1 scaling instead, set rescale = "MCMC": two methods generally produce different results, especially informative priors pDp_D deviates substantially qq.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: INLAvaan’s Bayesian fit indices follow mathematical framework blavaan (Merkle et al. 2021), implementation differences: Currently, INLAvaan supports pDp_D DIC (.e., p_dic). "ppmc" rescaling method (computes replicated data posterior predictive) yet available.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: well model predict new data? Leave-one-(LOO) cross-validation answers holding one unit time, refitting model remaining data, scoring held-unit refitted posterior. total score expected log predictive density, elpdloo=∑u=1nlog⁡p(yu∣y−u),   \mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{loo}} = \sum_{u=1}^n \log p(y_u \mid y_{-u}),  rewards models predict well automatically penalises overfitting – making natural criterion comparing models. Computed naively, LOO needs nn refits. MCMC-based packages blavaan avoid importance-sampling posterior draws (Vehtari et al. 2017), still requires full set MCMC draws. INLAvaan instead exploits Laplace machinery: loo() approximates case-deletion posterior Taylor expansion around full-data posterior summary, entire LOO computed single fit – refitting sampling. Holzinger–Swineford example takes fraction second. Two unit types scored, resolved automatically model: LOSO (leave-one-subject-): single-level models, one unit per row. LOCO (leave-one-cluster-): two-level models, one unit per cluster – relevant predictive question “well model predict new cluster?”.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: Write ℓu(θ)=log⁡p(yu∣θ)\ell_u(\theta) = \log p(y_u \mid \theta) unit uu’s log-likelihood contribution, score sus_u Hessian HuH_u evaluated posterior summary (θ*,Σ)(\theta^*, \Sigma) fit. loo() reports first- second-order Taylor approximations log conditional predictive ordinate: log⁡CPOu(1)=ℓu−12su⊤Σsu,log⁡CPOu(2)=ℓu−12su⊤(Σ−1+Hu)−1su+12log⁡|+ΣHu|. \begin{aligned}   \log \mathrm{CPO}_u^{(1)} &= \ell_u - \tfrac12 s_u^\top \Sigma\, s_u, \\   \log \mathrm{CPO}_u^{(2)} &= \ell_u     - \tfrac12 s_u^\top (\Sigma^{-1} + H_u)^{-1} s_u     + \tfrac12 \log \lvert + \Sigma H_u \rvert . \end{aligned}  headline elpd_loo sum second-order terms, standard error nvar̂(log⁡CPOu)\sqrt{n \, \widehat{\mathrm{var}}(\log \mathrm{CPO}_u)}, looic =−2elpdloo= -2\,\mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{loo}} familiar information-criterion scale. effective number parameters ploo=∑u(lpdu−log⁡CPOu)p_{\mathrm{loo}} = \sum_u (\mathrm{lpd}_u - \log \mathrm{CPO}_u) uses analogous expansion full-posterior pointwise predictive density lpdu\mathrm{lpd}_u. rare unit whose second-order curvature matrix positive definite falls back first order (flagged output); warning raised happens many units, since suggests Gaussian posterior summary poor.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: fit classic three-factor CFA Holzinger–Swineford data. Fitting meanstructure = TRUE recommended LOO: otherwise unit log-likelihoods evaluated zero means absolute ELPD values biased (comparisons models data remain valid either way). pointwise contributions available inspection – useful spotting influential observations (large score_norm unusually low log_cpo_2 flags unit model predicts poorly):

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: ELPD differences models fitted data paired, standard errors computed pointwise differences rather marginal SEs. compare(..., loo = TRUE) automatically: Models sorted descending ELPD; elpd_diff se_diff relative best model. common heuristic difference smaller couple se_diff units practically meaningful (Vehtari et al. 2017) – three-factor model preferred wide margin.
code: one.factor <- "g =~ x1 + x2 + x3 + x4 + x5 + x6 + x7 + x8 + x9" fit1f <- acfa(one.factor, HolzingerSwineford1939, meanstructure = TRUE,               verbose = FALSE)  compare(fit, fit1f, loo = TRUE) #> Bayesian Model Comparison (INLAvaan) #> Models ordered by ELPD (Taylor LOO) #> elpd_diff/se_diff are paired differences vs the best model #>  #>  Model npar Marg.Loglik    logBF      DIC     pD      ELPD     SE  p_loo #>    fit   30   -3885.211    0.000 7534.951 29.468 -3769.109 42.945 32.433 #>  fit1f   27   -3990.563 -105.352 7757.081 26.938 -3878.134 46.800 27.516 #>  elpd_diff se_diff #>      0.000   0.000 #>   -109.025  17.072

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: models fitted cluster argument, loo() automatically switches per-cluster scoring (LOCO). covariates modelled jointly (fixed.x = FALSE); next section explains happens lavaan’s default covariate treatment.
code: model2l <- "   level: 1     fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3     fw ~ x1 + x2 + x3   level: 2     fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3     fb ~ w1 + w2 " fit2l <- asem(model2l, Demo.twolevel, cluster = "cluster",               meanstructure = TRUE, fixed.x = FALSE, verbose = FALSE) #> Warning: Fit diagnostics flagged 2 potential issues: #> ✖ The optimiser did not converge: iteration limit reached without convergence #>   (10). #> ✖ The gradient at the posterior mode is not zero (max |grad| = 2.33): a Newton #>   step would move `y1~~y1.l2` by 0.169 posterior SDs. #> ℹ Inspect with `diagnostics(fit)` and `diagnostics(fit, type = "param")`.  loo(fit2l) #> Taylor leave-one-cluster-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 200 clusters (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate     SE #> elpd_loo -23344.2  731.4 #> p_loo        34.3    2.0 #> looic     46688.3 1462.9

what: Exogenous covariates: joint and conditional scores
title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: model contains exogenous covariates, flavour LOO score follows likelihood model fitted . fixed.x = FALSE covariates receive saturated Gaussian block unit scored joint predictive density outcomes covariates (“surprised model brand-new unit, characteristics included?”). fixed.x = TRUE – lavaan’s default – fitted likelihood conditional one, unit scored predictive density outcomes given covariates (“given new unit known characteristics, well predict outcomes?”), familiar regression cross-validation convention. refitting trickery involved: conditional likelihood exactly invariant fixed covariate moments, conditional score carries additional approximation. two flavours estimate different quantities whose scales differ covariate predictive density, joint conditional elpd must never appear comparison – compare(..., loo = TRUE) refuses mixed-flavour comparisons outright. Within conditional flavour, however, models conditioning different covariate sets directly comparable long outcome variables match, makes covariate selection straightforward: (joint flavour comparison require retaining grade models, since joint scores models spanning different variable sets live different sample spaces.) flavours support covariate placement, including cluster-level within-level covariates two-level models.
code: model_x <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9   visual ~ ageyr + grade   textual ~ ageyr + grade " dat_x <- na.omit(   HolzingerSwineford1939[, c(paste0("x", 1:9), "ageyr", "grade")] )  # lavaan's default fixed.x = TRUE: scored conditionally on the covariates fit_cond <- asem(model_x, dat_x, meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) #> Warning: Fit diagnostics flagged 1 potential issue: #> ✖ The fitted marginal deviates from the scanned posterior (NMAD > 0.1) for #>   `x2~1` (0.11), `x3~1` (0.10). #> ℹ Inspect with `diagnostics(fit)` and `diagnostics(fit, type = "param")`. loo(fit_cond) #> Taylor leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 300 subjects (second-order Taylor approximation) #> Scored conditionally on the exogenous covariates (fixed.x fit) #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo  -3748.1 44.7 #> p_loo        45.1  2.7 #> looic      7496.2 89.5 model_x1 <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9   visual ~ ageyr " fit_cond1 <- asem(model_x1, dat_x, meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)  compare(fit_cond, fit_cond1, loo = TRUE) #> Bayesian Model Comparison (INLAvaan) #> Models ordered by ELPD (Taylor LOO) #> elpd_diff/se_diff are paired differences vs the best model #>  #>      Model npar Marg.Loglik   logBF      DIC     pD      ELPD     SE  p_loo #>   fit_cond   32   -3875.892   0.000 7541.867 61.284 -3748.090 44.737 45.076 #>  fit_cond1   29   -3903.093 -27.201 7569.009 30.459 -3787.678 43.881 38.271 #>  elpd_diff se_diff #>      0.000   0.000 #>    -39.587  10.261

what: Storing the result with the fit
title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: loo(fit) never modifies fitted object – default test = "standard", fit already computes stores full LOO WAIC whenever model supported, mean structure, (LOO) predicted serial cost within 10-second budget. prediction calibrated run time timing single score evaluation, typical single-level models – full LOO costs fraction fit – simply get loo(fit) waic(fit) free. WAIC reuses draws fit produced posterior summaries, costs one casewise pass. expensive cases can force LOO regardless budget fit time, store afterwards explicit reassignment: stored result returned instantly loo(fit) reused fitMeasures() (blavaan-style names appear) compare(..., loo = TRUE):
code: fit <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, meanstructure = TRUE,             test = c("standard", "loo")) fit <- add_loo(fit) fitMeasures(fit, c("elpd_loo", "se_loo", "p_loo", "looic")) #>  elpd_loo     p_loo     looic    se_loo  #> -3769.109    32.433  7538.217    85.890

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: widely applicable information criterion (Watanabe 2010) asymptotically equivalent LOO also available. Unlike loo(), waic() sampling-based: evaluates unit log-likelihoods posterior draws, results carry Monte Carlo error, units pwaic,u>0.4p_{\mathrm{waic},u} > 0.4 trigger reliability warning – case prefer loo() (Vehtari et al. 2017).
code: set.seed(1) waic(fit) #> WAIC (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 1000 posterior draws and 301 subjects #>  #>           Estimate   SE #> elpd_waic  -3769.3 42.9 #> p_waic        32.1  2.2 #> waic        7538.5 85.9 #>  #> 8 units with p_waic > 0.4: the WAIC may be unreliable; prefer loo().

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: theta Sigma arguments evaluate LOO arbitrary Gaussian posterior summary instead fit’s . Combined Gaussian conditioning, scores constrained submodel encompassing fit alone. example, score submodel visual ~~ speed covariance fixed zero, condition summary parameter re-evaluate: refit took place: conditioned summary covariance locked zero (row column Sigma_c vanish), LOO machinery automatically restricts remaining parameters. pair arguments building block custom model-search strategies – screen many candidate restrictions conditioning, score loo(), refit winners. INLAvaan deliberately provides just evaluation API; search logic design.
code: int <- get_inlavaan_internal(fit) theta <- int$theta_star Sigma <- int$Sigma_theta  p <- which(names(coef(fit)) == "visual~~speed") theta_c <- theta - Sigma[, p] * (theta[p] / Sigma[p, p]) Sigma_c <- Sigma - tcrossprod(Sigma[, p]) / Sigma[p, p]  loo(fit, theta = theta_c, Sigma = Sigma_c) #> Taylor leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 301 subjects (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo  -3785.9 45.0 #> p_loo        34.0  2.5 #> looic      7571.9 90.1 #>  #> Evaluated at a user-supplied (theta, Sigma) summary.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: verify INLAvaan recovers correct parameters, simulate data “truth” known. logic follows: Generate… XX normally; MM dependent XX coefficient 0.5; YY dependent MM coefficient 0.7. Critically, add XX generation YY. means true direct effect (cc) 0, relationship fully mediated. expect model estimate ≈0.5a \approx 0.5, b≈0.7b \approx 0.7, indirect effect ab≈0.35ab \approx 0.35. direct effect cc close zero.
code: set.seed(11) n <- 100  # sample size  # 1. Predictor X <- rnorm(n)  # 2. Mediator (Path a = 0.5) M <- 0.5 * X + rnorm(n)  # 3. Outcome (Path b = 0.7, Path c = 0) Y <- 0.7 * M + rnorm(n)   dat <- data.frame(X = X, Y = Y, M = M)

title: Mediation Analysis
text: standard lavaan syntax mediation model straightforward (note use := operator define indirect effect new parameter.): model fit using asem(). meanstructure = TRUE argument supplied estimate intercepts variables. user may wish specify different prior distributions parameters. See relevant section Get started vignetted details.
code: mod <- "   # Direct effect (path c)   Y ~ c*X    # Mediator paths (path a and b)   M ~ a*X   Y ~ b*M    # Define Indirect effect (a*b)   ab := a*b    # Define Total effect   total := c + (a*b) " library(INLAvaan) fit <- asem(mod, dat, meanstructure = TRUE) #> ℹ Mode finding and Hessian computation. #> ✔ Posterior mode and Hessian. [43ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.084σ. [216ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/7 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fit 7/7 skew-normal marginals. [784ms] #>  #> ℹ Fit measures: PPP, DIC, LOO, WAIC.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: summary output provides posterior mean, standard deviation, 95% credible intervals paths. Looking Regressions Defined Parameters sections output: intercepts non-significant, since simulated data true means zero. Path aa (M ~ X) estimated 0.525 (true value 0.5). Path bb (Y ~ M) estimated 0.771 (true value 0.7). Path cc (Y ~ X) estimated -0.060. 95% Credible Interval [-0.291, 0.171] includes zero, correctly identifying direct effect. Indirect Effect abab estimated 0.406 (true value 0.35). interval [0.215, 0.597] cross zero, indicating significant mediation. sum direct indirect effects (c+abc + ab). tells us 1-unit increase XX leads total increase roughly 0.349 YY. Note: simulation, even though direct effect non-significant (close zero), total effect significant mechanism via MM strong. illustrates “full mediation” scenario: XX affects YY, MM.

title: Multigroup Analysis
text: Multigroup SEM fits model structure several groups , group keeping parameters unless told otherwise. classic application measurement invariance (Meredith 1993): instrument measure construct, scale, across groups? question asked ladder increasingly constrained models: Configural: factor structure every group, parameters free. Metric (weak): loadings constrained equal across groups – latent variables common scale, latent covariances can compared. Scalar (strong): loadings intercepts equal – observed scores map latent scores way, latent means can compared. rung fitted adding group.equal. use Holzinger–Swineford data, 301 children two schools (Pasteur Grant-White) took nine cognitive tests: Cross-group equality constraints handled exactly (penalty): constrained loadings become single free parameters shared groups. summary() fit reports parameters per group.
code: HS.model <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 " data("HolzingerSwineford1939", package = "lavaan")  fit_configural <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, group = "school",                        meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) fit_metric <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, group = "school",                    group.equal = "loadings",                    meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) fit_scalar <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, group = "school",                    group.equal = c("loadings", "intercepts"),                    meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)

title: Multigroup Analysis
text: compare() reports marginal log-likelihoods, Bayes factors, DIC: within-flavour comparisons (three fits model means proper priors), Bayes factors meaningful . marginal likelihoods answer prior-sensitive question – “model gave data highest prior predictive density?” – invariance testing usually want predictive one: rung ladder predicts new children best? compare(..., loo = TRUE) answers leave-one-cross-validation computed single fit – refitting, sampling (see LOO article machinery). Groups independent, every child scored school’s implied moments, expected log predictive density (ELPD) sums schools. Differences come paired standard errors, since children scored every model: textbook Holzinger–Swineford story appears: metric invariance indistinguishable configural (paired difference well within one standard error, equal-loadings model – simpler, predictive reach – preferred), scalar invariance costs two standard errors ELPD: intercepts genuinely differ schools. Note instructive disagreement Bayes factors, reward prior-predictive parsimony pick scalar model; predictive grounds ladder stops metric.

text: use Demo.twolevel dataset included R package lavaan, make easier follow, interpret variables within educational context. data contains following information: Clusters (cluster): 200 different schools. Observations: 2500 students nested within schools. Outcomes (y1, y2, y3): Three distinct survey items measuring “Academic Performance.” Within-level Predictors (x1, x2, x3): Student-specific factors Study Hours, Sleep Hours, Attendance. -level Predictors (w1, w2): School-level factors Teacher Experience, School Budget. model, assume two latent variables: fw measuring individual students aptitude (.e. student ability) fb measuring school quality, .e. shared variance performance attributable school environment. envisaged two-level SEM can visualized follows:

title: Multilevel SEM
text: First, load INLAvaan dataset.

title: Multilevel SEM
text: INLAvaan (following lavaan syntax), specify model level using level: <block> keywords. example, Level 1: define latent variable fw (Student Ability) regress individual predictors (x). Level 2: define latent variable fb (School Quality) regress school-level predictors (w). use asem() function (Approximate SEM) fit model. Crucially, must specify cluster argument identify grouping variable.
code: mod <- "   level: 1       # Measurement model (Within-student)       fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3       # Structural model: Individual predictors       fw ~ x1 + x2 + x3    level: 2       # Measurement model (Between-school)       fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3       # Structural model: School-level predictors       fb ~ w1 + w2 " fit <- asem(mod, data = Demo.twolevel, cluster = "cluster") #> ℹ Mode finding and Hessian computation. #> ✔ Posterior mode and Hessian. [719ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.123σ. [357ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/20 skew-normal marginals. #> ⠹ Fitting 9/20 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fit 20/20 skew-normal marginals. [2.7s] #>  #> ℹ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). #> ✔ Adjust copula correlations (NORTA). [109ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ⠹ Computing WAIC. #> ✔ Summarise 1000 posterior draws. [43.6s] #>  #> ℹ Fit measures: PPP, DIC, LOO, WAIC.

title: Prior and Posterior Predictive Checks
text: fit classic three-factor Holzinger–Swineford model draw 50 replicate datasets posterior predictive distribution. element yrep data frame dimensions original data. overlay density every replicate (grey) observed density (teal), nine indicators: Figure 1: Posterior predictive check: densities 50 replicate datasets (grey) observed data (teal) nine indicators. indicators sit comfortably inside replicate envelope. clearest misfit x3: observed density second bump right Gaussian replicate reproduces—feature data model generate. code: dat <- lavaan::HolzingerSwineford1939 mod <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 " fit <- acfa(mod, dat, verbose = FALSE) yrep <- simulate(fit, nsim = 50) vars <- paste0("x", 1:9) rep_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(yrep), function(s) {   d <- yrep[[s]][vars]   data.frame(sim = s, var = rep(vars, each = nrow(d)),              value = unlist(d, use.names = FALSE)) })) obs_df <- data.frame(var = rep(vars, each = nrow(dat)),                      value = unlist(dat[vars], use.names = FALSE))  ggplot() +   geom_density(data = rep_df, aes(value, group = sim),                colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.2) +   geom_density(data = obs_df, aes(value),                colour = "#00A6AA", linewidth = 0.9) +   facet_wrap(~var, scales = "free") +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Density") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

code: imp <- sampling(fit, type = "implied", nsamp = 2000, prior = TRUE) rho_def <- vapply(imp, \(m) cov2cor(m$cov)["x1", "x2"], numeric(1))  fit_tight <- acfa(mod, dat, dp = priors_for(lambda = "normal(0,1)"),                   verbose = FALSE) imp <- sampling(fit_tight, type = "implied", nsamp = 2000, prior = TRUE) rho_tight <- vapply(imp, \(m) cov2cor(m$cov)["x1", "x2"], numeric(1)) rho_df <- data.frame(   rho = c(rho_def, rho_tight),   prior = rep(c("lambda ~ normal(0,10)", "lambda ~ normal(0,1)"), each = 2000) )  ggplot(rho_df, aes(rho, fill = prior)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60,                  alpha = 0.5, position = "identity") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("lambda ~ normal(0,10)" = "#00A6AA",                                "lambda ~ normal(0,1)" = "#F18F00")) +   labs(x = expression(rho(x[1], x[2])), y = "Density", fill = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: fitting Bayesian SEM INLAvaan, often want draw samples model —either using posterior prior parameter values. sampling() function exactly : propagates parameter draws full generative chain 𝛉⏟parameters→𝛈⏟latent variables→𝐲*⏟observed variables \underbrace{\boldsymbol\theta}_{\text{parameters}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\boldsymbol\eta}_{\text{latent variables}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\mathbf{y}^*}_{\text{observed variables}} producing samples tied individual observation. distinct predict(), returns individual-specific factor scores 𝛈∣𝐲,𝛉\boldsymbol\eta \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol\theta. Typical use cases include posterior predictive checks (PPCs) prior predictive checks; predictive checks article walks workflow.

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: INLAvaan offers two ways draw data generative model, difference varies draws: sampling() refreshes 𝛉\boldsymbol\theta every draw, pooling parameter uncertainty sampling variability single marginal predictive distribution. simulate() holds 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} fixed whole dataset, variation across replicates reflects parameter uncertainty variation within replicate reflects sampling variability—makes replicates exchangeable observed data, hence right tool predictive checks simulation-based calibration. functions accept prior = TRUE. rest article covers sampling().

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: Let 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} (s=1,…,Ss = 1, \dots, S) denote one parameter draw. draw SEM matrices 𝚲\boldsymbol\Lambda, 𝚿\boldsymbol\Psi, 𝐁\mathbf{B}, 𝛂\boldsymbol\alpha, 𝛎\boldsymbol\nu, 𝚯\boldsymbol\Theta constructed. generative chain : 1. Latent variables. 𝛈(s)∼𝒩((𝐈−𝐁)−1𝛂,𝚽),𝚽=(𝐈−𝐁)−1𝚿[(𝐈−𝐁)−1]′. \boldsymbol\eta^{(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(   (\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol\alpha,\;   \boldsymbol\Phi \bigr), \qquad \boldsymbol\Phi = (\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol\Psi\,   [(\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}]'. 2. Observed variables. 𝐲*(s)∼𝒩(𝚲𝛈(s)+𝛎,𝚯). \mathbf{y}^{*(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(   \boldsymbol\Lambda\,\boldsymbol\eta^{(s)} + \boldsymbol\nu,\;   \boldsymbol\Theta \bigr). prior = FALSE (default), 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} comes posterior; prior = TRUE, parameter drawn independently prior.

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: default type "lavaan" returns S×pS \times p matrix lavaan-side (constrained) parameter draws:
code: dat <- lavaan::HolzingerSwineford1939 mod <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 " fit <- acfa(mod, dat, verbose = FALSE) theta_post <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 2000) dim(theta_post) #> [1] 2000   21 head(theta_post[, 1:4]) #>      visual=~x2 visual=~x3 textual=~x5 textual=~x6 #> [1,]  0.6217858  0.9061140   1.1243541   0.9963907 #> [2,]  0.5512358  0.8050023   1.2136311   0.9163359 #> [3,]  0.5589911  0.7004874   0.9801615   0.9248339 #> [4,]  0.5567474  0.7638705   1.1415682   1.0361746 #> [5,]  0.5948947  1.0233424   1.0731154   0.8664639 #> [6,]  0.3630961  0.7067411   1.0889999   0.8696213 eta  <- sampling(fit, type = "latent",   nsamp = 1000) ystar <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 1000) dim(eta)    # 1000 x 3 (one per latent factor) #> [1] 1000    3 dim(ystar)  # 1000 x 9 (one per observed indicator) #> [1] 1000    9 all_samps <- sampling(fit, type = "all", nsamp = 1000) names(all_samps) #> [1] "lavaan"   "theta"    "latent"   "observed" "implied"

what: Comparing samples to the fitted marginals
title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: INLAvaan approximates marginal posterior skew-normal density. can overlay sampling() histogram (drawn via copula method) top fitted density curve stored pdf_data verify agree. Figure 1: Copula samples (histogram) versus fitted skew-normal marginal (red curve) free parameters.
code: # 1. Draw copula samples samp_cop <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 1000, samp_copula = TRUE)  # 2. Retrieve the fitted skew-normal densities int <- fit@external$inlavaan_internal pdf_data <- int$pdf_data  # 3. Build a long data frame for ggplot par_names <- colnames(samp_cop) hist_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_cop)),   value = as.vector(samp_cop) ) hist_df$param <- factor(hist_df$param, levels = par_names)  curve_df <- do.call(rbind, Map(function(nm, df) {   data.frame(param = nm, x = df$x, y = df$y) }, par_names, pdf_data[par_names])) curve_df$param <- factor(curve_df$param, levels = par_names)  # 4. Plot ggplot(hist_df, aes(x = value)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  fill = "grey40", alpha = 0.5) +   geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = y),             colour = "#00A6AA", linewidth = 0.8) +   facet_wrap(~param, scales = "free") +   labs(x = "Parameter value", y = "Density",        title = "Copula samples vs. fitted skew-normal posterior") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: default, sampling() uses copula method, respects skew-normal marginals. Setting samp_copula = FALSE uses multivariate Gaussian (Laplace) approximation instead. difference visible parameters asymmetric posteriors, variance components. Figure 2: Comparison marginal histograms copula sampling (blue) Gaussian sampling (orange) parameters. symmetric posteriors (e.g., factor loadings), two methods nearly identical. positively-skewed posteriors (e.g., residual variances), copula method captures asymmetry Gaussian approximation symmetric construction.
code: samp_gauss <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 5000, samp_copula = FALSE)  cop_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_cop)),   value = as.vector(samp_cop),   method = "Copula" ) gauss_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_gauss)),   value = as.vector(samp_gauss),   method = "Gaussian" ) both_df <- rbind(cop_df, gauss_df) both_df$param <- factor(both_df$param, levels = par_names) both_df$method <- factor(both_df$method, levels = c("Copula", "Gaussian"))  ggplot(both_df, aes(x = value, fill = method)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  alpha = 0.45, position = "identity") +   facet_wrap(~param, scales = "free") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(Copula = "#00A6AA", Gaussian = "#F18F00")) +   labs(x = "Parameter value", y = "Density", fill = "Method",        title = "Copula vs. Gaussian posterior sampling") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis
text: Efficient approximate Bayesian inference Structural Equation Models. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods remain gold standard exact Bayesian inference, can prohibitively slow iterative model development. INLAvaan offers rapid alternative latent variable analysis, delivering Bayesian results (near) speed frequentist estimators. achieves custom, ground-implementation Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA), engineered specifically lavaan modelling framework.

title: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis
text: INLAvaan designed fit seamlessly existing workflow. familiar (b)lavaan syntax, can begin using INLAvaan immediately. first impression package, consider canonical example SEM applied Industrialisation Political Democracy data set Bollen (1989)1:
code: library(INLAvaan) mod_poldem <- "   # Latent variable definitions      ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3      dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3      dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8    # Latent regressions     dem60 ~ ind60     dem65 ~ ind60 + dem60    # Residual correlations     y1 ~~ y5     y2 ~~ y4 + y6     y3 ~~ y7     y4 ~~ y8     y6 ~~ y8      # Fixed loading     dem60 =~ 1.5*y4      # Custom priors on latent variances     ind60 ~~ prior('gamma(1, 1)')*ind60     dem60 ~~ prior('gamma(2, 1)')*dem60     dem65 ~~ prior('gamma(1,.5)')*dem65 " utils::data("PoliticalDemocracy", package = "lavaan")  fit <- asem(model = mod_poldem, data = PoliticalDemocracy) #> ℹ Mode finding and Hessian computation. #> ✔ Posterior mode and Hessian. [95ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.157σ. [105ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/30 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fit 30/30 skew-normal marginals. [895ms] #>  #> ℹ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). #> ✔ Adjust copula correlations (NORTA). [110ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ✔ Summarise 1000 posterior draws. Set TRUE show . priors Logical; TRUE, include prior information estimates. nd Integer; number decimal places print numeric values.

what: INLAvaan: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis — INLAvaan-package
title: INLAvaan: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis — INLAvaan-package
text: Implements approximate Bayesian inference Structural Equation Models (SEM) using custom adaptation Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (Rue et al., 2009) doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2008.00700.x  described Jamil Rue (2026a) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2603.25690 . Provides computationally efficient alternative Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) Bayesian estimation, allowing users fit latent variable models using 'lavaan' syntax. See also companion paper implementation workflows, Jamil Rue (2026b) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2604.00671 .

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Confirmatory Factor Analysis Model — acfa
text: model description user-specified model. Typically, model     described using lavaan model syntax. See     model.syntax information. Alternatively,     parameter table (e.g., output lavParTable() function) also     accepted. data optional data frame containing observed variables used     model. variables declared ordered factors, lavaan     treat ordinal variables. dp Default prior distributions different types model parameters; named character vector returned priors_for(). test Character indicating post-estimation quantities compute. Defaults "standard": posterior fit indices (PPP DIC), plus – models supported casewise machinery fitted mean structure – WAIC (reusing fit's posterior draws, nsamp >= 100) full leave-one-cross-validation whenever predicted serial cost within 10-second budget; stored fit (see loo() waic()). "none" skips . Include "loo" (e.g. test = c("standard", "loo"), test = "loo" alone) force full LOO regardless budget. vb_correction Logical indicating whether apply variational Bayes correction posterior mean vector estimates. Defaults TRUE. marginal_method method approximating marginal posterior distributions. Options include "skewnorm" (skew-normal), "asymgaus" (two-piece asymmetric Gaussian), "marggaus" (marginalising Laplace approximation), "sampling" (sampling joint Laplace approximation). marginal_correction type correction use fitting skew-normal two-piece Gaussian marginals. "hessian" computes full "shortcut" (default) computes diagonals via central differences (full z-trace plus Schur complement correction), "shortcut_fd" formula using forward differences (roughly half cost, less accurate), "hessian" computes full Hessian-based correction (slow), "none" (FALSE) applies correction. nsamp number samples draw sampling-based approaches (including posterior sampling model fit indices). samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals (e.g. skew-normal asymmetric Gaussian), NORTA correlation adjustment. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation. re sn_fit_ngrid Number grid points lay per dimension fitting skew-normal marginals. finer grid gives better fit cost joint-log-posterior evaluations. Defaults 21. sn_fit_logthresh log-threshold fitting skew-normal. Points log-posterior drop threshold (relative maximum) excluded fit. Defaults -6. sn_fit_temp Temperature parameter fitting skew-normal. Defaults 1 (weights density values ). NA, temperature included additional optimisation parameter. sn_fit_sample Logical. TRUE (default), parametric skew-normal fitted posterior samples covariance defined parameters. FALSE, summarised using kernel density estimation instead. control list control parameters optimiser. verbose Logical indicating whether print progress messages. debug Logical indicating whether return debug information. add_priors Logical indicating whether include prior densities posterior computation. optim_method optimisation method use finding posterior mode. Options include "nlminb" (default), "ucminf", "optim" (BFGS). numerical_grad Logical indicating whether use numerical gradients optimisation. Defaults FALSE use analytical gradients. cores Integer NULL. Number cores parallel marginal fitting. NULL (default), serial execution used unless number free parameters exceeds 120, case parallelisation enabled automatically using available physical cores. Set 1L force serial execution. cores > 1, marginal fits distributed across cores using parallel::mclapply() (fork-based; parallelism Windows). ... Additional arguments passed lavaan model fitting function.

title: Compare Bayesian Models Fitted with INLAvaan — compare
text: first argument x serves baseline (null) model. models (including baseline) appear comparison table. baseline also passed fitMeasures() incremental fit indices (BCFI, BTLI, BNFI) requested via fit.measures. default table always includes: npar: Number free parameters. Marg.Loglik: Approximated marginal log-likelihood. logBF: Natural-log Bayes factor relative best model. DIC / pD: Deviance Information Criterion effective number parameters (test != "none" used fitting). Set fit.measures character vector measure names (anything returned fitMeasures()) append extra columns. Use fit.measures = "" include every available measure. Set loo = TRUE compare models leave-one-cross-validation (see loo()). appends ELPD / SE (second-order Taylor expected log predictive density standard error), p_loo, , best-ELPD model, difference elpd_diff paired standard error se_diff computed pointwise contributions (appropriate uncertainty nested -data comparisons). table sorted descending ELPD. models must fitted data matching units; units paired id rather row order, fits stack groups differently – pooled fit multigroup fit, multigroup fits different group orderings – still pair unit unit. missing-data (FIML) fits, "data" also means observed entries: unit scored entries , comparisons require identical missingness patterns across models. models must also share score flavour (see loo()): mixing fits modelled covariates (fixed.x = FALSE, joint scores) fixed covariates (fixed.x = TRUE, conditional scores) refused. Joint scores additionally require identical variable sets across models, conditional scores require matching outcome variables – covariate sets may differ, covariate-selection setting. Stored LOO results (test = "loo" add_loo()) reused. anova() disabled INLAvaan fits – direct Bayesian analogue classical likelihood-ratio test – points instead.

title: Convergence and Approximation Diagnostics for INLAvaan Models — diagnostics
text: type = "global", named numeric vector (class "diagnostics.INLAvaan"). type = "param", data frame (class c("diagnostics.INLAvaan.param", "data.frame")).

title: Convergence and Approximation Diagnostics for INLAvaan Models — diagnostics
text: Global diagnostics (type = "global"): npar Number free parameters. nsamp Number posterior samples drawn. converged 1 optimiser converged, 0 otherwise. iterations Number optimiser iterations. grad_inf L-infinity norm analytic gradient mode (max |grad|). ~0 convergence. grad_inf_rel Relative L-infinity norm analytic gradient (max |grad| / (|par| + 1e-6)). grad_l2 L2 (Euclidean) norm analytic gradient mode. mode_shift_max Maximum, across parameters, Newton step reported mode posterior-SD units (max |\(\Sigma_\theta\) grad| / se). Unlike raw gradient norms scale-free: estimates far reported mode sits true posterior mode relative posterior uncertainty. ~0 convergence. hess_cond Condition number Hessian (precision matrix) computed \(\Sigma_\theta\). Large values indicate near-singularity. vb_kld_global Global KL divergence VB mean correction (NA VB correction applied). vb_applied 1 VB correction applied, 0 otherwise. kld_max Maximum per-parameter KL divergence VB correction. kld_mean Mean per-parameter KL divergence. nmad_max Maximum normalised max-absolute-deviation across marginals (skew-normal method ; NA otherwise). nmad_mean Mean NMAD across marginals. Per-parameter diagnostics (type = "param"): data frame columns: param Parameter name. grad Analytic gradient negative log-posterior mode. ~0 convergence. grad_num Numerical (finite-difference) gradient mode. agree grad; large discrepancies indicate bug analytic gradient. grad_diff Difference grad_num - grad: ~0. grad_abs Absolute analytic gradient. grad_rel Relative analytic gradient |grad| / (|par| + 1e-6). mode_shift_sigma Newton step reported mode posterior-SD units. ~0 convergence. kld Per-parameter KL divergence VB correction. vb_shift VB correction shift (original scale). vb_shift_sigma VB shift units posterior SD. nmad Normalised max-absolute-deviation skew-normal fit (NA using skewnorm method). Fit-time warnings: inlavaan() runs checks end every fit emits single consolidated warning (condition class "inlavaan_diagnostics_warning") look : optimiser converge, mode_shift_max exceeds 0.1, marginal NMAD 0.1, VB correction shifted posterior mean 1 posterior SD, Hessian condition number exceeds 1e8. healthy fit stays silent. Silence check suppressWarnings(), selectively handling condition class.

title: Fit a skew normal distribution to log-density evaluations — fit_skew_normal
code: # Fit a SN curve to gamma log-density logdens <- function(x) dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 1, log = TRUE)  x_grid <- seq(0.1, 8, length.out = 21) y_log  <- sapply(x_grid, logdens) y_log  <- y_log - max(y_log)  # normalise to have maximum at zero  res <- fit_skew_normal(x_grid, y_log, temp = 10) unlist(res) #>           xi        omega        alpha         logC            k         rmse  #>  0.842445393  2.539226101  3.310505376  1.332422700 10.000000000  0.002109188  #>         nmad  #>  0.173298470   # Compare truth vs skew-normal approximation x_fine <- seq(0.1, 8, length.out = 200) y_true <- exp(logdens(x_fine)) y_sn   <- dsnorm(x_fine, xi = res$xi, omega = res$omega, alpha = res$alpha)  plot(x_fine, y_true, type = "n", xlab = "x", ylab = "Density", bty = "n") polygon(c(x_fine, rev(x_fine)), c(y_true, rep(0, length(x_fine))),         col = adjustcolor("#131516", 0.25), border = NA) lines(x_fine, y_sn, col = "#00A6AA", lwd = 2) legend("topright", legend = c("Truth", "SN Approx."),        fill = c(adjustcolor("#131516", 0.25), NA), border = NA,        col = c(NA, "#00A6AA"), lwd = c(NA, 2), lty = c(NA, 1),        bty = "n")

what: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Model — inlavaan
title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Model — inlavaan
text: function fits Bayesian latent variable model approximating posterior distributions model parameters using various methods, including skew-normal, asymmetric Gaussian, marginal Gaussian, sampling-based approaches. leverages lavaan package model specification estimation.

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Model — inlavaan
code: inlavaan(   model,   data,   model.type = "sem",   dp = priors_for(),   test = "standard",   vb_correction = TRUE,   marginal_method = c("skewnorm", "asymgaus", "marggaus", "sampling"),   marginal_correction = c("shortcut", "shortcut_fd", "hessian", "none"),   nsamp = 1000,   samp_copula = TRUE,   sn_fit_ngrid = 21,   sn_fit_logthresh = -6,   sn_fit_temp = 1,   sn_fit_sample = TRUE,   control = list(),   verbose = TRUE,   debug = FALSE,   add_priors = TRUE,   optim_method = c("nlminb", "ucminf", "optim"),   numerical_grad = FALSE,   start = NULL,   cores = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Model — inlavaan
text: model description user-specified model. Typically, model     described using lavaan model syntax. See     model.syntax information. Alternatively,     parameter table (e.g., output lavParTable() function) also     accepted. data optional data frame containing observed variables used     model. variables declared ordered factors, lavaan     treat ordinal variables. model.type lavaan entry point used fit model: "cfa", "sem", "growth" (matching lavaan's model-specific wrapper functions), "lavaan" general-purpose interface. Set automatically acfa(), asem(), agrowth(); documented explicitly lavaan >= 0.7-1 renamed corresponding simulateData() argument model_type, can longer inherited . dp Default prior distributions different types model parameters; named character vector returned priors_for(). test Character indicating post-estimation quantities compute. Defaults "standard": posterior fit indices (PPP DIC), plus – models supported casewise machinery fitted mean structure – WAIC (reusing fit's posterior draws, nsamp >= 100) full leave-one-cross-validation whenever predicted serial cost within 10-second budget; stored fit (see loo() waic()). "none" skips . Include "loo" (e.g. test = c("standard", "loo"), test = "loo" alone) force full LOO regardless budget. vb_correction Logical indicating whether apply variational Bayes correction posterior mean vector estimates. Defaults TRUE. marginal_method method approximating marginal posterior distributions. Options include "skewnorm" (skew-normal), "asymgaus" (two-piece asymmetric Gaussian), "marggaus" (marginalising Laplace approximation), "sampling" (sampling joint Laplace approximation). marginal_correction type correction use fitting skew-normal two-piece Gaussian marginals. "hessian" computes full "shortcut" (default) computes diagonals via central differences (full z-trace plus Schur complement correction), "shortcut_fd" formula using forward differences (roughly half cost, less accurate), "hessian" computes full Hessian-based correction (slow), "none" (FALSE) applies correction. nsamp number samples draw sampling-based approaches (including posterior sampling model fit indices). samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals (e.g. skew-normal asymmetric Gaussian), NORTA correlation adjustment. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation. re sn_fit_ngrid Number grid points lay per dimension fitting skew-normal marginals. finer grid gives better fit cost joint-log-posterior evaluations. Defaults 21. sn_fit_logthresh log-threshold fitting skew-normal. Points log-posterior drop threshold (relative maximum) excluded fit. Defaults -6. sn_fit_temp Temperature parameter fitting skew-normal. Defaults 1 (weights density values ). NA, temperature included additional optimisation parameter. sn_fit_sample Logical. TRUE (default), parametric skew-normal fitted posterior samples covariance defined parameters. FALSE, summarised using kernel density estimation instead. control list control parameters optimiser. verbose Logical indicating whether print progress messages. debug Logical indicating whether return debug information. add_priors Logical indicating whether include prior densities posterior computation. optim_method optimisation method use finding posterior mode. Options include "nlminb" (default), "ucminf", "optim" (BFGS). numerical_grad Logical indicating whether use numerical gradients optimisation. Defaults FALSE use analytical gradients. start Optional numeric vector starting values optimiser, given full vector free parameters internal (unconstrained) parameterisation. Mainly internal use update(), warm-starts mode-finding previous fit's posterior mode; supplying hand-built vector requires knowledge internal parameter ordering. length must equal number free parameters error raised. cores Integer NULL. Number cores parallel marginal fitting. NULL (default), serial execution used unless number free parameters exceeds 120, case parallelisation enabled automatically using available physical cores. Set 1L force serial execution. cores > 1, marginal fits distributed across cores using parallel::mclapply() (fork-based; parallelism Windows). ... Additional arguments passed lavaan model fitting function.

title: Log-Likelihood for INLAvaan Models — logLik
text: marginal log-likelihood already integrates (Laplace- approximated) posterior, scale classical log-likelihood passed AIC()/BIC() – double-penalise model complexity evidence already accounted . Use compare() compare models via Bayes factors, DIC, LOO/WAIC. plug-variant exists users specifically want point-estimate-based classical comparison. AIC()/BIC() INLAvaan fit disabled (mirroring anova()): large-sample asymptotic approximations quantities INLAvaan already computes directly – AIC approximates predictive accuracy, loo()/waic() already estimate rigorously; BIC approximates \(-2\) times log marginal likelihood, logLik() already returns directly (Laplace approximation). Point-estimate AIC/BIC remain available reporting-convention purposes via AIC(logLik(object, type = "plugin")) / BIC(logLik(object, type = "plugin")).

text: Computes leave-one-(LOO) cross-validation fitted INLAvaan model single fit, refitting sampling, via Taylor approximation case-deletion posterior around Laplace summary. code: # \donttest{ HS.model <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 " utils::data("HolzingerSwineford1939", package = "lavaan") fit <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, meanstructure = TRUE) #> ℹ Mode finding and Hessian computation. #> ✔ Posterior mode and Hessian. [146ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.146σ. [105ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/30 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fit 30/30 skew-normal marginals. [744ms] #>  #> ℹ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). #> ✔ Adjust copula correlations (NORTA). [132ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ✔ Summarise 1000 posterior draws. [1.2s] #>  #> ℹ Fit measures: PPP, DIC, LOO, WAIC.  # Leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) from the single fit -- no refitting res <- loo(fit) res #> Taylor leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 301 subjects (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo  -3769.1 43.0 #> p_loo        32.5  2.2 #> looic      7538.2 86.0 head(res$per_unit) #>   unit nobs    l_star score_norm     lpd_1     lpd_2 log_cpo_1 log_cpo_2 #> 1    1    1 -17.30842   6.635871 -17.19447 -17.24144 -17.42237 -17.47301 #> 2    2    1 -13.76823   5.418304 -13.68502 -13.75552 -13.85143 -13.92648 #> 3    3    1 -11.10669   3.789637 -11.07093 -11.16168 -11.14245 -11.23559 #> 4    4    1 -10.25020   2.575335 -10.23618 -10.28284 -10.26422 -10.31190 #> 5    5    1 -10.70254   2.975327 -10.68907 -10.73622 -10.71601 -10.76423 #> 6    6    1 -13.36953   4.983693 -13.30783 -13.39522 -13.43123 -13.52280 #>      det_term   ok #> 1 -0.04901313 TRUE #> 2 -0.07241546 TRUE #> 3 -0.09242052 TRUE #> 4 -0.04760200 TRUE #> 5 -0.04816499 TRUE #> 6 -0.08964209 TRUE  # Score a submodel without refitting: condition the Laplace summary on the # visual ~~ speed covariance being zero, then evaluate at that summary int <- get_inlavaan_internal(fit) theta <- int$theta_star Sigma <- int$Sigma_theta p <- which(names(coef(fit)) == "visual~~speed") theta_c <- theta - Sigma[, p] * (theta[p] / Sigma[p, p]) Sigma_c <- Sigma - tcrossprod(Sigma[, p]) / Sigma[p, p] loo(fit, theta = theta_c, Sigma = Sigma_c) #> Taylor leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 301 subjects (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo  -3786.3 45.0 #> p_loo        34.0  2.5 #> looic      7572.5 90.0 #>  #> Evaluated at a user-supplied (theta, Sigma) summary.  # Two-level models are scored per cluster (LOCO) automatically utils::data("Demo.twolevel", package = "lavaan") model2l <- "   level: 1     fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3     fw ~ x1 + x2 + x3   level: 2     fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3     fb ~ w1 + w2 " fit2l <- asem(model2l, Demo.twolevel, cluster = "cluster",               meanstructure = TRUE, fixed.x = FALSE) #> ℹ Mode finding and Hessian computation. #> ℹ Computing the Hessian. #> ✔ Posterior mode and Hessian. [869ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.092σ. [453ms] #>  #> ⠙ Fitting 0/34 skew-normal marginals. #> ⠹ Fitting 11/34 skew-normal marginals. #> ⠸ Fitting 24/34 skew-normal marginals. #> ✔ Fit 34/34 skew-normal marginals. [7.5s] #>  #> ℹ Adjusting copula correlations (NORTA). #> ✔ Adjust copula correlations (NORTA). [130ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ⠹ Computing fit indices (PPP/DIC). #> ⠸ Computing WAIC. #> ✔ Summarise 1000 posterior draws. [46s] #>  #> ℹ Fit measures: PPP, DIC, LOO, WAIC. #> Warning: Fit diagnostics flagged 2 potential issues: #> ✖ The optimiser did not converge: iteration limit reached without convergence #>   (10). #> ✖ The gradient at the posterior mode is not zero (max |grad| = 2.33): a Newton #>   step would move `y1~~y1.l2` by 0.169 posterior SDs. #> ℹ Inspect with `diagnostics(fit)` and `diagnostics(fit, type = "param")`. loo(fit2l) #> Taylor leave-one-cluster-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 200 clusters (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate     SE #> elpd_loo -23344.2  731.4 #> p_loo        34.3    2.0 #> looic     46688.3 1462.9 # }

Example: "gamma(1,0.5)[sd]" places Gamma(1, 0.5) prior \(\sigma = \sqrt{\text{variance}}\). [prec]: Prior precision \(\tau = 1/\sigma^2\). Example: "gamma(1,1)[prec]" places Gamma(1, 1) prior \(\tau = 1/\text{variance}\). parameterisation used blavaan corresponds Inverse-Gamma prior variance. necessary Jacobian adjustment applied automatically cases.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: fast approximation skew-normal quantiles using high-performance approximation algorithm INLA GMRFLib C source, originally Thomas Luu (see details reference).

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
code: qsnorm_fast(p, xi = 0, omega = 1, alpha = 0)

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: p Vector probabilities. xi Location parameter (numeric vector). omega Scale parameter (numeric vector). alpha Shape parameter (numeric vector).

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: Vector quantiles.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: function implements high-performance approximation skew-normal quantile function based algorithm described Luu (2016). method uses domain decomposition strategy achieve high accuracy (\(< 10^{-7}\) relative error) without iterative numerical inversion. domain split two regions: Tail Regions: extreme probabilities \(\vert u \vert\) large, quantile approximated using Lambert W-function, \(W(z)\), solving \(z = \Phi(q)\) via asymptotic expansion: $$q \approx \sqrt{2 W\left(\frac{1}{2\pi (1-p)^2}\right)}$$ Central Region: main body distribution, function uses high-order Taylor expansion inverse error function around carefully selected expansion point $x_0$: $$\Phi^{-1}(p) \approx \sum_{k=0}^5 c_k (z - x_0)^k$$ approach significantly faster standard numerical inversion (e.g., uniroot) maintaining sufficient precision statistical applications.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: Luu, T. (2016). Fast accurate parallel computation quantile functions random number generation (Doctoral thesis). UCL (University College London). https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1482128/

title: Widely Applicable Information Criterion for INLAvaan Models — waic
text: posterior draw \(\theta_s\) (drawn copula sampler used fit's posterior summaries) unit \(u\), log-likelihood \(\log p(y_u \mid \theta_s)\) evaluated; \(\mathrm{lpd}_u = \log \tfrac{1}{S}\sum_s p(y_u \mid \theta_s)\), \(p_{\mathrm{waic},u} = \mathrm{var}_s \log p(y_u \mid \theta_s)\), \(\mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{waic}} = \sum_u (\mathrm{lpd}_u - p_{\mathrm{waic},u})\) \(\mathrm{WAIC} = -2\, \mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{waic}}\). Unlike loo(), sampling-based estimate: results vary random draws, units \(p_{\mathrm{waic},u} > 0.4\) trigger reliability warning (also annotated printing). model restrictions loo() apply, flavour rule: fits fixed.x = TRUE scored conditionally exogenous covariates, fits fixed.x = FALSE jointly (see loo()). Marginal vs conditional WAIC (two-level models). default per-cluster scoring marginal WAIC, corresponds leave-one-cluster-cross-validation – prediction new cluster. Setting type = "loso" scores conditional WAIC, corresponding leave-one-unit-– prediction new observation within observed cluster (row contributes conditional density observed entries given rest cluster). two answer different questions easily conflated (Merkle, Furr & Rabe-Hesketh, 2019); per-cluster marginal usual model-comparison target, default, type = "loso" warns. matches loo(type = "loso") – two compute estimand sampling Taylor expansion. default test = "standard", inlavaan() computes WAIC fit time reusing posterior draws fit already produced (model supported nsamp >= 100), stores fit: waic(fit) returns stored result called default arguments, fitmeasures() reports waic, p_waic, se_waic part "" free. loo package attached masks generic, dispatch INLAvaan objects continues work.

WAIC reuses fit’s posterior draws (nsamp >= 100), LOO runs predicted serial cost within 10-second budget. test = "loo" forces LOO regardless budget, test = "none" skips everything, fit <- add_loo(fit) stores post hoc. Stored results reused loo(), waic(), fitmeasures(), compare(). fitted() (fitted.values()) return model-implied moments INLAvaan fit, evaluated posterior means, matching lavaan blavaan output structure. type = "ov" gives casewise predicted values. predict() gains summary argument; summary = TRUE collapses posterior draws returns point estimates directly, equivalent summary(predict(...)) one call. Default FALSE, existing code unaffected. residuals() (resid()) return observed-minus-fitted moments INLAvaan fit, matching lavaan blavaan output structure supporting lavaan residual types (raw, cor, cor.bentler, normalized, standardized) plus type = "casewise". anova() INLAvaan fit now errors, pointing compare(). Unlike fitted()/residuals()/predict(), deliberate departure blavaan (silently inherits lavaan’s frequentist likelihood-ratio test): direct Bayesian analogue test, compare() already provides appropriate tools (Bayes factors, DIC/pD, LOO/WAIC). logLik() returns Laplace-approximated marginal log-likelihood (log evidence) default, printed note comparable classical log-likelihood; type = "plugin" instead returns classical log-likelihood posterior mean, df/nobs attributes supports AIC()/BIC() point estimate. deviance() new INLAvaan fits (lavaan deviance() ). Follows BUGS/JAGS/Stan convention: type = "mean" (default) returns posterior mean deviance pD/DIC attached attributes; type = "plugin" returns deviance posterior mean (matching -2 * logLik(type = "plugin")). require test != "none". AIC()/BIC() INLAvaan fit now error, documented alongside logLik(). large-sample asymptotic approximations quantities INLAvaan already computes directly – AIC approximates predictive accuracy (loo()/waic()), BIC approximates -2 * log(marginal likelihood) (logLik()) – reporting posterior mean cruder proxy numbers already available. point estimate remains available reporting-convention purposes via AIC(logLik(object, type = "plugin")) / BIC(...). Fits now self-check diagnostics: inlavaan() warns , end fit, optimiser converge, gradient reported mode materially non-zero (Newton step > 0.1 posterior SD), skew-normal marginal fits poorly (NMAD > 0.1), VB correction shifted posterior mean 1 posterior SD, Hessian near-singular – naming offending parameters. healthy fit stays silent; suppress via inlavaan_diagnostics_warning condition class. diagnostics() gains scale-free mode_shift_max (global) mode_shift_sigma (per-parameter) measures backing gradient check. (#18)

title: INLAvaan 0.3.0
text: Saturated-means fast path: mean structure saturated (intercepts free unconstrained normal priors, nonzero latent means), posterior exactly block-diagonal intercepts covariance parameters mode. Hessian intercept block now computed analytically exact zero cross block (finite differences run covariance columns ), skew-normal marginal scans skip intercept axes, emitting exact Gaussian marginals directly. 25% faster typical CFA/SEM fits, results identical within finite-difference noise. Improved messaging inlavaan() fit calls.

title: INLAvaan 0.3.0
text: standardisedsolution() summary(standardized = TRUE) longer silently drop arguments lavaan >= 0.7-1, renamed several exported arguments (e.g. cov.std cov_std, GLIST glist). INLAvaan now resolves spelling installed lavaan expects per session load, working across lavaan versions. Two-level FIML loo()/waic() scores now correct clusters containing case fully missing within-level variables. lavaan retains cases analytic gradient kernel mishandles zero-observed pattern; INLAvaan drops rows cluster kernels (exact marginal likelihood). Two-level FIML fitting also inherits upstream gradient issue (fixed lavaan PR #581), inlavaan() warns cases present lavaan versions fix. saturated means given flat priors marginalised analytically (closed form), replacing lavaan’s profiled likelihood, valid Bayesian object. Posterior modes recalibrate factor n/(n-1) covariance side. loo() waic() score fits exact exchangeable case-deletion conditionals. previous zero-mean fallback warning gone, absolute ELPD values meaningful comparable meanstructure = TRUE fits. Posterior predictive draws include saturated means mean-uncertainty. Requesting meanstructure = FALSE two-level model now warns fits meanstructure = TRUE (mean structure required ). conditional (fixed.x = TRUE) flavour — default SEM exogenous covariates — fully supported: mean marginalisation factorises blockwise, unit scored difference two exchangeable conditionals, frozen-covariate term entering exact constant. predict() now centres conditioning data model-implied means (saturated sample means model mean structure) drawing factor scores predicted observed variables. Previously kernels conditioned raw data, offsetting every factor score constant grows variable means. sampling() simulate() draws observed variables models without mean structure now include saturated (sample) means, posterior predictive replicates live data scale instead centred zero. sampling() simulate() longer error models single latent variable, saturated-mean recovery now robust missing data (replicate columns previously NA missing = "pairwise"). PPP’s observed discrepancy now uses unbiased (divisor n-1) sample covariance, matching scale Wishart-replicated covariances compared ; previously divisor-n form made PPP slightly optimistic (O(1/n) effect, models). coef() (merged parameter table, fitted values, implied moments) now reports covariance parameters covariance scale. Previously slots carried posterior-mean correlation, summary() showed correct sample-based covariance; discrepancy visible whenever relevant standard deviations far 1.

Summary statistics also available via fitmeasures(). compare() compares two fitted models side side, reporting marginal log-likelihood, Bayes factors, DIC, optional fit measures fitmeasures(). diagnostics() computes global per-parameter convergence approximation-quality diagnostics fitted models. get_inlavaan_internal() now exported documented, providing access internal list stored fitted INLAvaan object. predict() generates predictions observed data missing data imputation, respecting multilevel structure present. sampling() draws posterior (prior) SEM generative model, returning parameter vectors, latent variables, observed variables. simulate() generates complete replicate datasets fitted model, useful simulation-based calibration posterior predictive checks. timing() extracts wall-clock timings individual computation stages fitted model.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.4
text: Cholesky factorisation precision matrix replaces raw solve() covariance log-determinant calculations. Copula sampling NORTA (NORmal Anything) correlation adjustment now default (samp_copula = TRUE), ensuring posterior samples correct skew-normal marginals correct Pearson correlations.
